sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: Occult primary tumors (cancers of unknown primary [CUP]) are histologically confirmed metastatic tumors whose primary site cannot be identified during standard pretreatment evaluation. These are heterogeneous tumors with a wide variety of clinical presentations and generally poor prognosis. The management philosophy is individualized, multidisciplinary, and goal-directed, focusing on symptom control and quality of life, especially in disseminated disease. The majority of CUP (approximately 80%) have a poor prognosis with a median overall survival (OS) of 3 to 10 months. However, certain favorable prognostic subsets exist, including patients with a single small resectable tumor, poorly differentiated carcinoma with midline nodal distribution, squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) of cervical lymph nodes, isolated inguinal adenopathy (SCC), poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinomas, women with papillary adenocarcinoma of the peritoneal cavity, women with adenocarcinoma involving only axillary lymph nodes, and men with blastic bone metastases and elevated prostate-specific antigen (PSA). For these patients, tailored treatment approaches, such as locoregional therapies or specific systemic therapy regimens, are recommended and may prolong survival.

nodes.label: CUP Diagnosis (histologically confirmed metastatic tumor with unknown primary)
Multidisciplinary evaluation (MDT, pathologist for IHC/molecular testing, radiologist, oncologists)
Determine treatment intent based on disease extent and prognosis
Localized disease amenable to locoregional therapy or favorable prognostic subset
Disseminated disease, poor prognosis (majority, median OS 3–10 months)
PS good (0-1): Curative treatment: surgical resection, RT, +/- systemic therapy based on likely primary site
PS poor: Consider palliative measures; treatment individualized
Assess performance status and symptom burden (for palliative intent)
Symptomatic PS 1-2 or asymptomatic PS 0 with aggressive cancer: Systemic therapy (palliative, e.g., FOLFIRINOX for PS 0-1)
PS poor (3-4): Palliative RT, supportive care, symptom control
Specialized approaches: targeted therapies, novel RT

edges.label: Symptomatic PS 1-2 or asymptomatic PS 0 aggressive

cards.title: Intent
cards.detail:
Intent: Curative

Patient group: Patients with localized disease amenable to locoregional therapy, such as a single resectable metastasis or limited nodal disease.

Approach: Treatment with curative intent may involve surgical resection, radiation therapy (RT), or both, often in conjunction with systemic therapy, and is based on the most likely primary site as guided by workup findings.

cards.title: Intent
cards.detail:
Intent: Palliative

Patient group: The majority of patients with disseminated metastases and a poor prognosis.

Approach: Treatment goals are symptom control, quality of life, and potentially prolonging survival. Systemic therapy is generally palliative. Preferred options include enrollment in a clinical trial, consideration of systemic therapy on an individual basis, and specialized approaches (e.g., targeted therapies, novel RT, palliative care).
